refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005 Refined: The NGHE solution shall automatically derive performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) from worksite mission context and operational parameters, record them in a traceable baseline, and maintain a managed history of all requirement updates and calibration changes with full audit trail.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR was vague on 'method' and lacked explicit traceability and history management. Refinement adds specificity: (1) automatic derivation from mission context, (2) explicit baseline recording, (3) managed history with audit trail. This aligns with acceptance criteria demanding documented performance targets traceable to worksite inputs and maintained baselines/calibration updates. High safety criticality demands explicit safety margin derivation and audit capability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verification shall confirm: (a) performance requirements are automatically computed from worksite mission inputs (e.g., terrain, payload, cycle count); (b) baseline is recorded with timestamp, source, and derivation method; (c) history log captures all updates with reason, timestamp, and approver; (d) traceability chain from worksite input → derived requirement → baseline → history is complete and auditable; (e) safety margins are explicitly calculated and documented.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: Ingest worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints) and operational parameters.
CR-NGHE-005.2: Compute performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) using mission-context-aware algorithms.
CR-NGHE-005.3: Record derived performance requirements as a baseline with metadata (timestamp, source, derivation method, approver).
CR-NGHE-005.4: Maintain a managed history of all performance requirement baselines and calibration updates with full audit trail (who, what, when, why).
CR-NGHE-005.5: Provide traceability links from worksite inputs → derived requirements → baseline records → history entries.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized Performance Derivation Engine – A dedicated software module that ingests worksite mission context via standardized APIs, applies mission-aware algorithms to compute performance targets, records baselines in a versioned database, and maintains an immutable audit log of all changes.

DP-B: Distributed Derivation with Local Caching – Performance derivation logic embedded in Vehicle and Teleoperation modules independently, with periodic synchronization to a central history store; allows offline computation but risks inconsistency.

DP-C: Manual Requirement Entry with Post-Hoc Validation – Site Manager manually enters performance targets via UI; system validates against mission context and records history; low automation, high human error risk.

DP-D: Hybrid Centralized + Safety-Critical Validation – Centralized derivation engine computes targets; Safety Services module independently validates safety margins and approves baseline before recording; ensures safety oversight.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Hybrid Centralized + Safety-Critical Validation – A centralized Performance Derivation Engine (in NGHE Solution) computes performance requirements from worksite mission context using mission-aware algorithms. Safety Services module independently validates that safety margins meet worksite safety plan constraints and approves the baseline. Upon approval, the baseline is recorded with full metadata and audit trail in a versioned requirement store. All subsequent updates trigger re-validation by Safety Services before history entry.

potential_sources: DP-D couples the Performance Derivation Engine (NGHE Solution) with Safety Services validation logic. This is intentional and justified: safety criticality (high) demands that safety margins are independently verified before baseline acceptance. The coupling is unidirectional (Derivation Engine → Safety Services for approval) and well-defined via SafetyAlert interface.
DP-D decouples from Vehicle and Teleoperation modules, avoiding distributed logic and inconsistency risks present in DP-B.
DP-D avoids manual entry errors of DP-C by automating derivation from mission context.
coupling_check.notes: The coupling between Derivation Engine and Safety Services is a deliberate design choice to satisfy high safety criticality. It is not a flaw but a feature. The interface is explicit (SafetyAlert for approval signals) and the dependency is manageable.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite mission context is available in a structured, machine-readable format (e.g., JSON, standardized schema) at system initialization or via Tasking interface.
ASSUMPTION: Mission-aware algorithms for deriving cycle time, energy efficiency, and availability from terrain, payload, and operational constraints are well-defined and validated.
ASSUMPTION: Safety Services module has the expertise and data to independently validate safety margins against worksite safety plan.
RISK: If mission context is incomplete or malformed, derivation may produce invalid requirements. Mitigation: Implement input validation and fallback to manual entry with warnings.
RISK: If Safety Services validation is slow or unavailable, baseline recording is blocked. Mitigation: Implement timeout and escalation to Site Manager.
RISK: Versioned requirement store may become a bottleneck if update frequency is high. Mitigation: Implement caching and asynchronous audit logging.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-D is sound. Recommend clarifying the interface contract between Derivation Engine and Safety Services: (1) What data does Safety Services require for validation? (2) What is the approval SLA? (3) What happens if validation fails—does the system suggest corrective actions or escalate to Site Manager? These details should be captured in the DP-IM interface specification.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module owns the Performance Derivation Engine, which ingests worksite mission context, applies mission-aware algorithms to compute performance requirements, records baselines in a versioned requirement store, and maintains an immutable audit log. The module also owns the requirement store (database) and audit log infrastructure. Safety Services module is a separate owner responsible for safety margin validation and approval.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints) from Site Manager or Tele-operator via Tasking interface. This is the primary input to the Performance Derivation Engine.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution sends derived performance requirements (with safety margins) to Safety Services via SafetyAlert for validation and approval. Safety Services returns approval/rejection signals via SafetyAlert. This is a critical control loop for high safety criticality.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution publishes baseline performance requirements and history records via Telemetry interface for consumption by Vehicle, Teleoperation, and monitoring dashboards. This enables downstream systems to align their operations with approved performance targets.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution uses Network Link for persistent storage of requirement baselines and audit logs, ensuring data durability and offline access capability.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of the Performance Derivation Engine and requirement store. The module is system-level and responsible for mission-context-aware logic. Containment is high because derivation, baseline recording, and audit logging are core system functions. Diffusion risk is minimal (5%) because the logic is centralized and not scattered across Vehicle or Teleoperation. Minor diffusion: Telemetry publication requires coordination with Vehicle/Teleoperation for consumption, but this is a standard interface dependency, not logic diffusion.

Vehicle module is responsible for vehicle-level control and telemetry, not system-level performance derivation. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would scatter mission-context-aware logic into a vehicle-specific module, creating diffusion. Vehicle should consume approved performance targets from NGHE Solution, not derive them. Containment is low (15%) because Vehicle lacks the system-level context and mission-aware algorithms needed for derivation.

Teleoperation module is responsible for remote operation and tele-operator interface, not performance requirement derivation. Assigning DP-D to Teleoperation would create a distributed derivation logic that conflicts with centralized baseline management. Containment is low (20%) because Teleoperation lacks the mission-context-aware algorithms and system-level authority to derive and approve performance targets. Teleoperation should consume approved targets from NGHE Solution.

Safety Services is responsible for safety validation and approval, which is a critical part of DP-D. However, Safety Services should not own the Performance Derivation Engine itself; it should own the validation and approval logic. Assigning the entire DP-D to Safety Services would conflate derivation (a system-level function) with safety validation (a safety-specific function). Containment is moderate (40%) because Safety Services owns the validation gate, but not the derivation engine. Recommended: NGHE Solution owns derivation engine; Safety Services owns validation/approval logic. This is a split ownership model with clear interface (SafetyAlert).

assigned_im.ownership_candidate: NGHE Solution (primary owner of Performance Derivation Engine and requirement store); Safety Services (co-owner of safety margin validation and approval logic).
assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP-D is best realized by a split ownership model: (1) NGHE Solution owns the Performance Derivation Engine, requirement store, and audit log infrastructure. (2) Safety Services owns the safety margin validation and approval logic, invoked via SafetyAlert interface. This split respects module boundaries: NGHE Solution is system-level and mission-aware; Safety Services is safety-specific and validation-focused. The interface between them (SafetyAlert) is explicit and manageable.
containment_risks: RISK: If NGHE Solution and Safety Services have misaligned definitions of 'safety margin,' derived requirements may fail validation. Mitigation: Establish a shared data model and validation criteria document, reviewed by both teams.
RISK: If SafetyAlert interface is slow or unreliable, baseline recording is delayed. Mitigation: Implement timeout and escalation; consider caching of last-approved baseline.

RISK: If Safety Services validation logic is complex and evolves frequently, NGHE Solution may need to update its interface contract. Mitigation: Use versioned SafetyAlert interface and maintain backward compatibility.
RISK: Audit log may grow large if update frequency is high, impacting storage and query performance. Mitigation: Implement log rotation, archival, and indexed queries.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the split ownership model in the architecture document: (1) Define the SafetyAlert interface contract in detail (data schema, approval SLA, error handling). (2) Establish a shared data model for performance requirements and safety margins. (3) Define the audit log schema and retention policy. (4) Specify the fallback behavior if Safety Services validation fails or times out. (5) Consider implementing a requirement versioning system to support rollback and comparison of baselines.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (Hybrid Centralized + Safety-Critical Validation) directly addresses all sub-FRs of CR-NGHE-005 Refined: (1) Ingestion of worksite mission context via Tasking interface. (2) Computation of performance requirements using mission-aware algorithms in the Derivation Engine. (3) Recording of baselines with metadata in the requirement store. (4) Maintenance of managed history with audit trail. (5) Traceability from worksite inputs to history entries via the Derivation Engine and SafetyAlert validation loop. DP-D is traceable, feasible, and aligned with high safety criticality by including Safety Services validation. No coupling issues or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK (LOW): Performance Derivation Engine logic is centralized in NGHE Solution, avoiding scatter across Vehicle or Teleoperation. However, Telemetry publication requires coordination with downstream consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation). This is a standard interface dependency, not logic diffusion. Mitigation: Define Telemetry schema and publication contract clearly.

DIFFUSION RISK (LOW): Safety margin validation logic is owned by Safety Services, not NGHE Solution. This is intentional and reduces diffusion by separating concerns. However, it introduces a dependency on SafetyAlert interface. Mitigation: Implement robust error handling and timeout logic in NGHE Solution.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution containment score is 95%, indicating strong fit. The module is system-level and responsible for mission-context-aware logic. Safety Services containment score is 40% for the validation/approval logic, which is appropriate because Safety Services is a specialized module focused on safety, not system-level derivation. The split ownership model (NGHE Solution + Safety Services) respects module boundaries and avoids diffusion. External interfaces (Tasking, SafetyAlert, Telemetry, Network Link) are all known and well-defined. No new interfaces required.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-005 Refined is feasible: (1) Automated tests can verify that the Derivation Engine correctly computes performance requirements from mission context inputs. (2) Integration tests can verify that baselines are recorded with correct metadata and audit trail. (3) Traceability tests can verify that worksite inputs are linked to derived requirements and history entries. (4) Safety validation tests can verify that Safety Services approves/rejects baselines correctly. (5) Audit log tests can verify that all updates are recorded with full context. Verification does not require new test infrastructure beyond standard software testing practices.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns the Performance Derivation Engine, requirement store, and audit log. Safety Services owns the validation/approval logic. Interfaces are explicit (Tasking, SafetyAlert, Telemetry, Network Link). Ownership boundaries are well-defined. No ambiguity or overlap detected.

recommended_revisions: Clarify the SafetyAlert interface contract: (a) Data schema for derived requirements and safety margins. (b) Approval SLA and timeout behavior. (c) Error handling and escalation procedures. (d) Versioning and backward compatibility strategy.

Define the requirement store schema: (a) Baseline record structure (timestamp, source, derivation method, approver, safety margins). (b) Audit log entry structure (who, what, when, why, approval status). (c) Indexing and query strategy for traceability.
Establish a shared data model for performance requirements and safety margins between NGHE Solution and Safety Services.

Specify the fallback behavior if Safety Services validation fails or times out: (a) Should the system escalate to Site Manager? (b) Should it use the last-approved baseline? (c) Should it block operations until validation succeeds?
Consider implementing a requirement versioning system to support rollback, comparison, and audit trail analysis.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-005 Refined is well-defined, DP-D is sound and traceable, and IM assignment (NGHE Solution + Safety Services) is clear and feasible. All gates pass. However, the refined FR has five sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-005.1 through CR-NGHE-005.5) that require further decomposition to reach atomic, implementable requirements. Specifically: (1) CR-NGHE-005.1 (Ingest mission context) needs to specify the mission context schema and ingestion protocol. (2) CR-NGHE-005.2 (Compute requirements) needs to specify the algorithms and their inputs/outputs. (3) CR-NGHE-005.3 (Record baseline) needs to specify the baseline schema and metadata. (4) CR-NGHE-005.4 (Maintain history) needs to specify the audit log schema and retention policy. (5) CR-NGHE-005.5 (Provide traceability) needs to specify the traceability links and query interface. These sub-FRs are not yet atomic and require further decomposition at the next level.
decision.justification_detail: The decision to ZIGZAG_DOWN is based on: (1) FR Refinement is complete and well-structured with clear sub-FRs. (2) DP Selection is sound and addresses all sub-FRs without coupling issues. (3) IM Assignment is clear with high containment scores and explicit interfaces. (4) Gate Review passes all three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM). (5) However, the sub-FRs are not yet atomic and require further decomposition to reach implementable requirements. Proceeding to the next level will refine each sub-FR into more specific, testable requirements and corresponding DPs and IMs.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: Ingest worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints) and operational parameters.
CR-NGHE-005.2: Compute performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) using mission-context-aware algorithms.
CR-NGHE-005.3: Record derived performance requirements as a baseline with metadata (timestamp, source, derivation method, approver).
CR-NGHE-005.4: Maintain a managed history of all performance requirement baselines and calibration updates with full audit trail (who, what, when, why).
CR-NGHE-005.5: Provide traceability links from worksite inputs → derived requirements → baseline records → history entries.

assumptions: Worksite mission context is available in a structured, machine-readable format at system initialization or via Tasking interface.
Mission-aware algorithms for deriving cycle time, energy efficiency, and availability from terrain, payload, and operational constraints are well-defined and validated.
Safety Services module has the expertise and data to independently validate safety margins against worksite safety plan.
SafetyAlert interface is reliable and has acceptable latency for baseline approval.
Requirement store (database) is available and has sufficient capacity for baseline and audit log storage.
Site Manager and Tele-operator can provide worksite mission context in the required format.
open_risks: If mission context is incomplete or malformed, derivation may produce invalid requirements. Mitigation: Implement input validation and fallback to manual entry with warnings.
If Safety Services validation is slow or unavailable, baseline recording is blocked. Mitigation: Implement timeout and escalation to Site Manager.

If NGHE Solution and Safety Services have misaligned definitions of 'safety margin,' derived requirements may fail validation. Mitigation: Establish a shared data model and validation criteria document.
Audit log may grow large if update frequency is high, impacting storage and query performance. Mitigation: Implement log rotation, archival, and indexed queries.
If requirement versioning is not implemented, rollback and comparison of baselines may be difficult. Mitigation: Consider implementing a versioning system.
evidence_needed: Worksite mission context schema and examples (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints).
Mission-aware algorithms for deriving cycle time, energy efficiency, and availability (mathematical models, validation data).
SafetyAlert interface specification (data schema, approval SLA, error handling).
Requirement store schema (baseline record structure, audit log entry structure, indexing strategy).
Shared data model for performance requirements and safety margins (agreed by NGHE Solution and Safety Services teams).
Fallback behavior specification (what happens if Safety Services validation fails or times out).
Test cases for Performance Derivation Engine (unit tests, integration tests, traceability tests, safety validation tests, audit log tests).
